schools.summary: Having shown the Jew under sin and circumcision profitless without obedience (ch. 2), Paul raises the natural objection — what advantage has the Jew, what profit circumcision? — a rhetorical question in diatribe style that he will answer (v.2, Robertson).
Calvin: having seemingly stripped the Jews of all dignity, Paul asks whether they had any advantage — to answer that it lay in God's free gift (the oracles), not their own merit.

themes: what advantage then hath the Jew
what profit is there of circumcision
the anticipated objection
the Jew's advantage

schools.summary: The Jew's advantage was much in every way, chiefly that the oracles of God (logia, the Scriptures of the Old Testament — law, prophets, Messianic promises) were committed to them, the supreme privilege of being the depositories and dispensers of God's word (Barnes, Robertson, Theodoret).
Calvin: the value lay in the word, not the bare sign — the oracles committed by God's free favour, not Israel's merit; our ingratitude is the worse if we receive God's word carelessly.

themes: much every way
unto them were committed the oracles of God
the Jew's chief advantage
the supreme privilege of the Scriptures

schools.summary: Paul raises the objection — if some (not all, a softening, Robertson, Theodoret) Jews were unbelieving or unfaithful (apisteo means both), does their unbelief make void 'the faith of God' (his faithfulness to his promises, Robertson, Barnes)?
Calvin: God's truth is firm and constant, not destroyed by man's perfidy; though most broke the covenant, it retained its efficacy for the believing remnant who received the promise by faith.

themes: what if some did not believe
shall their unbelief make the faith of God without effect
the objection from Israel's unbelief
God's faithfulness unshaken

schools.summary: Paul vehemently denies ('God forbid,' me genoito) that God's faithfulness could fail, affirming the great principle — let God be true though every man be a liar (echoing Psalm 116:11, Gill, Barnes) — and proves it from Psalm 51:4: God is justified in his sayings and overcomes when judged.

themes: God forbid: let God be true, but every man a liar
that thou mightest be justified in thy sayings
the vindication of God's truth
God true though every man fail

schools.summary: The objector argues that if our unrighteousness commends (sets off by contrast) God's righteousness, then God is unjust to take vengeance (Barnes, Robertson) — a sophistical attempt to escape judgment; Paul adds 'I speak as a man,' marking it a human, carnal cavil, not his own view.
Calvin: the perverse reasoning that would make God's glory an excuse for sin — Paul rejects it as blasphemous.

themes: if our unrighteousness commend the righteousness of God
is God unrighteous who taketh vengeance
the objection that sin magnifies God
I speak as a man

schools.summary: Paul vehemently rejects the objection ('God forbid,' me genoito) by a reductio — if God were unjust to punish sin, he could not judge the world at all; yet it is axiomatic that God will judge the world (Barnes, Calvin, JFB), so the objection destroys itself.

themes: God forbid
how shall God judge the world
the rejection of the objection
God's certain righteous judgment

schools.summary: The objector presses the cavil in the first person — if my falsehood (sin) makes God's truth abound more to his glory, why am I still judged as a sinner? (Barnes, Robertson) — the perverse reasoning that would make sin a benefit and condemnation unjust, which Paul restates to expose.

themes: if the truth of God hath more abounded through my lie
why yet am I also judged as a sinner
the objection sharpened
sin perversely made an excuse

schools.summary: Paul's opponents slanderously reported that he taught 'let us do evil that good may come' — the antinomian distortion of his doctrine of grace (Barnes, Robertson), the absurd conclusion of the cavil; and Paul pronounces 'whose damnation is just,' a stern, curt verdict on those who hold or charge such doctrine (Calvin, Robertson).

themes: as we be slanderously reported
let us do evil, that good may come
whose damnation is just
Paul's abhorrence of antinomianism

schools.summary: Paul asks whether the Jews are better than the Gentiles regarding justification, and answers 'No, in no wise' (ou pantos) — for he has already charged (proetiasametha) both Jews (ch. 2) and Gentiles (ch. 1) with being all under sin, under its guilt, power, and condemnation (Robertson).

Chrysostom and Theodoret: the universal verdict — all, Jew and Gentile, are under sin, justly arraigned before God's tribunal, the law of nature and the written law alike weighing down those who used them not aright.

themes: are we better than they? No, in no wise
all under sin
the universal verdict
Jew and Gentile leveled before God

schools.summary: Paul, to prove all are under sin, opens a catena of Old Testament quotations (chiefly Psalms and Isaiah, beginning Psalm 14) with the comprehensive, exceptionless verdict — 'none righteous, no, not one' — proving from the Jews' own revered Scriptures that all are sinners (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: as it is written
there is none righteous, no, not one
the scriptural catena on universal sin
the exceptionless verdict

schools.summary: The catena continues (Psalm 14:2) — none understands (lacks spiritual discernment, the mind darkened) and none seeks after God (the will averse, fleeing rather than seeking, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin); the corruption of both mind and will.

themes: there is none that understandeth
there is none that seeketh after God
the corruption of mind and will
the natural man blind and averse

schools.summary: The catena continues (Psalm 14:3) — all have gone out of the way (apostasy, wandering from God), all become unprofitable (useless, corrupt), none does good, no, not one (Barnes, Robertson); the total depravity of mankind apart from grace.
Cyril and Theodoret: the universal verdict repeated — not one does good, the emphatic exclusion of any exception, the corruption total.

themes: they are all gone out of the way
there is none that doeth good, no, not one
the universal corruption
total depravity apart from grace

schools.summary: The catena describes corrupt speech (Psalm 5:9; 140:3) — the throat an open sepulchre (exhaling corruption and death), the tongue used for deceit, the poison of asps under the lips (deadly, slanderous words, Barnes, Robertson, Theodoret).

themes: their throat is an open sepulchre
the poison of asps is under their lips
the corruption of human speech
the tongue's wickedness

schools.summary: The catena continues (Psalm 10:7) — the mouth full of cursing and bitterness, the speech of the wicked overflowing with imprecation (against God and men) and rancor, the overflow of a corrupt heart (Barnes, Ellicott).

themes: whose mouth is full of cursing and bitterness
the malice of corrupt speech
imprecation and rancor
the overflow of a corrupt heart

schools.summary: The catena turns to violent deeds (Isaiah 59:7; Proverbs 1:16) — the feet swift to shed blood, the eager readiness of the wicked to commit violence and murder, hastening to harm (Barnes, Robertson); the progression from corrupt speech to violent action.

themes: their feet are swift to shed blood
violent deeds
eager readiness for harm
sin from heart to mouth to deed

schools.summary: The catena continues (Isaiah 59:7) — destruction and misery in their ways, the wreckage the wicked leave behind, sin's path strewn with ruin (to others) and wretchedness (Barnes, Calvin).

themes: destruction and misery are in their ways
the ruinous fruit of sin
wreckage and wretchedness
sin's bitter harvest

schools.summary: The catena continues (Isaiah 59:8) — the way of peace they have not known, the wicked strangers to true peace, both with God and among men, given to violence rather than peace (Barnes, Calvin).

themes: the way of peace have they not known
strangers to peace
sin's alienation
no peace with God or men

schools.summary: The catena climaxes (Psalm 36:1) with the root and summary of all the foregoing sins — no fear of God before their eyes; the absence of the fear of God the fountain from which all corruption of mind, speech, and deed flows (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).
Henry: where no fear of God is, no good is to be looked for — the fear of God the beginning of wisdom, its absence the root of all evil (with Calvin).

themes: there is no fear of God before their eyes
the climax of the catena
the root of all sin
the absence of the fear of God

schools.summary: The law speaks to those under it (the Jews especially) — so the Jew cannot evade the indictment — that every mouth may be stopped (all excuse silenced) and all the world become guilty before God (Jew and Gentile brought under condemnation, Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): the law brings the whole world under guilt — all arraigned, all silenced, all guilty before God, that the righteousness by faith might then be sought.

themes: what things soever the law saith, it saith to them under the law
that every mouth may be stopped
all the world guilty before God
the convicting purpose of the law

schools.summary: No flesh (no one, Jew or Gentile) can be justified before God by the deeds of the law, for the law's office is to give the knowledge of sin, not justification — the law a mirror exposing guilt (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke, Ellicott).

Calvin: 'deeds of the law' means the whole law (moral included), not merely ceremonies (against Chrysostom, Origen, Jerome) — for the sins charged were against the moral law; whoever is found a sinner is deprived of righteousness, and partial righteousness by works is a frivolous figment.

debates.question: Do 'the deeds of the law' mean the moral law or only ceremonies?

sides.position: The whole law, moral included — the sins charged were moral, and no law can justify a transgressor
Both moral and ceremonial precepts, but neither can justify the doer

themes: by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified
by the law is the knowledge of sin
no justification by works
the law's convicting function

schools.summary: The emphatic 'but now' (nun de) — having proved all under sin, Paul sets forth the righteousness of God apart from law: God's plan of justifying men, not by personal obedience but by another way (the righteousness of 1:17 unfolded), yet witnessed by the law and the prophets, no novelty (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Theodoret (with Henry): this righteousness, though apart from law, was foretold and attested by the law and the prophets — the law had come to an end yet consented to grace, both bearing witness to it (Habakkuk, the sacrifices, the promises).

themes: the righteousness of God without the law is manifested
being witnessed by the law and the prophets
the great turn to grace
justification apart from works, foretold in Scripture

schools.summary: 'Faith of Jesus Christ' means faith in Jesus Christ (the objective genitive, as 'faith of God' = faith in God, Robertson, Barnes, Calvin) — not Christ's own faith, but faith directed to him as object, the instrument not the merit; the righteousness unto all and upon all that believe, Jew and Gentile, with no difference.

debates.question: Does 'faith of Jesus Christ' mean Christ's faith or faith in Christ?

sides.position: Faith in Christ — the objective genitive, faith directed to Christ as its object

themes: the righteousness of God which is by faith of Jesus Christ
unto all and upon all them that believe
there is no difference
faith in Christ the instrument of justification

schools.summary: All have sinned (the aorist gathering the whole race, in Adam and in their own persons, Robertson, Gill) and come short of the glory of God — most taking 'glory of God' as his praise or approbation (John 12:43, Barnes, Calvin, Robertson), the present tense marking the continuing deficiency; the universal ground of the leveling.

themes: for all have sinned
come short of the glory of God
the universal ground of the leveling
all dependent on grace

schools.summary: Believers are justified freely (dorean, a free undeserved gift, not earned) by God's grace (unmerited favour, the moving cause) through the redemption (apolutrosis, a releasing by ransom, the price paid to free captives) that is in Christ Jesus (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke, Wesley).

Calvin: the causes — God's mercy the efficient, Christ's blood the meritorious, faith the instrumental, God's glory the final; Theodoret: Christ offered his own body as the price of redemption, demanding faith only from us.

themes: being justified freely by his grace
through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus
justification a free gift
redemption by ransom

schools.summary: God set Christ forth (publicly exhibited on the cross) as the propitiation through faith in his blood, to declare his righteousness for the passing-over (paresin, distinct from remission) of past sins committed under God's forbearance; the atonement vindicating God's righteousness — Robertson leaning to 'propitiatory gift/sacrifice,' Barnes and Clarke to the mercy-seat.

Theodoret: the propitiatory was the golden mercy-seat over the ark; Christ is the true propitiatory of which that was a type — the name applied to him as man, not as God; God exhibited his mercy in long bearing with sinners while preparing this means of salvation.

Calvin: 'set forth' may mean foreordained or publicly exhibited; God is propitious to us as soon as our trust rests on Christ's blood; the passing-over of past sins refers to the legal expiations that could not pacify God — the reality suspended until Christ; God's righteousness here is his administrative justice, vindicated by the atonement.

debates.question: Does 'propitiation' (hilasterion) mean the mercy-seat or a propitiatory sacrifice?

sides.position: The mercy-seat — Christ the true mercy-seat where God is reconciled (the LXX usage)
A propitiatory sacrifice or gift — that which renders God propitious (suits 'in his blood' and 'set forth publicly')

themes: whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation through faith in his blood
to declare his righteousness for the remission of sins that are past
the great atonement passage
God propitious through Christ's blood

schools.summary: The heart of the gospel: God declares his righteousness now (the gospel age) that he might be just and the justifier of him who believes in Jesus — God remaining just (upholding his law, not pardoning without atonement) while justifying the believer; the cross alone reconciling justice and mercy (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

Calvin: God's righteousness here has two parts — that God alone is just (containing all fullness of righteousness) and that he communicates righteousness, justifying us by faith in Christ; the repetition emphatic, that the Jews might open their eyes to this demonstration now made in Christ.

themes: that he might be just, and the justifier of him which believeth in Jesus
God's righteousness declared at this time
the central crux of the gospel
justice and mercy meeting at the cross

schools.summary: Since justification is by grace through faith, all boasting (the Jews' self-glorying in works and privilege) is excluded, completely shut out — not by a law of works (which would leave room for self-glory) but by the law (principle) of faith, which receives all from God and brings nothing of its own (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).
Calvin (with Henry): justification by faith forever shuts out boasting, leaving not a particle of merit; faith ascribes all to God's mercy and brings only an humble confession of want.

themes: where is boasting then? It is excluded
by the law of faith
boasting shut out
all ascribed to God's mercy

schools.summary: Paul draws the great conclusion (logizometha, we hold) — a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law; the grand peculiarity of Christianity, Luther's 'article on which the church stands or falls' (Barnes), works not the ground of justification though its fruit.

Calvin: justification is so gratuitous it cannot be associated with the merit of works; James treats a different question (how faith is proved genuine, using 'justify' of proving), not how we are justified before God — so James and Paul agree, using the words in different senses.

debates.question: Does Paul's 'justified by faith without works' contradict James?

sides.position: No — James asks how faith is proved genuine (justify = prove), Paul how we are accounted righteous before God; different questions, different senses of 'justify'

themes: a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law
the cardinal doctrine of justification
the article on which the church stands or falls
the harmony with James

schools.summary: The Jews supposed God belonged to them alone, but Paul asks whether God is not also the God of the Gentiles, answering 'Yes, of the Gentiles also' — the inference from justification by faith: since it is by faith (not law or privilege), it is open to all, so God is the God of all (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: God designs to manifest himself as Savior to the Gentiles too, extending righteousness and salvation to all nations — having put all on a level, opening the kingdom of Christ to the whole world.

themes: is he the God of the Jews only
is he not also of the Gentiles? Yes
the universality of God and his salvation
the Gentiles included by faith

schools.summary: The unity of God grounds the unity of salvation — since there is one God, he justifies both Jew (circumcision) and Gentile (uncircumcision) the same way, by faith; 'by faith' (ek pisteos) and 'through faith' (dia tes pisteos) mean essentially the same, faith as source and means (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, Clarke).

Calvin: the variation may be ironical — if any wishes a difference between Jew and Gentile, let him take this, the one justified 'by faith,' the other 'through faith'; but it is the same faith, the same way, the distinction absurd.

themes: seeing it is one God
which shall justify the circumcision by faith, and uncircumcision through faith
the oneness of God and salvation
one way of justification for all

schools.summary: To the objection whether justification by faith makes void the law (the antinomian charge), Paul vehemently denies ('God forbid') and answers that faith establishes the law — God showing regard for it in requiring atonement, Christ magnifying and fulfilling it, and the justified believer led by faith working through love to obey it (Barnes, Ellicott).

Clarke: a guard against antinomianism — faith works by love, and love is the principle of obedience; whoever receives salvation by faith receives power to live in holy obedience to every moral precept; the law established both ceremonially (fulfilled in Christ) and morally.

themes: do we then make void the law through faith? God forbid
yea, we establish the law
faith establishes, not voids, the law
the guard against antinomianism
